int binaryGap(int n) {
    vector<int> positions;
    for (int i = 0; i < 32; ++i) {
        if (n & (1 << i)) {
            positions.push_back(i);
        }
    }
    if (positions.size() < 2) return 0;
    int maxDiff = 0;
    for (int i = 1; i < positions.size(); ++i) {
        int diff = positions[i] - positions[i-1];
        if (diff > maxDiff) {
            maxDiff = diff;
        }
    }
    return maxDiff;
}

Time complexity: O(1). Because the loop runs 32 times, which is constant. The second loop runs O(k) times, where k is the number of 1s. But since k can be at most 32 (for all bits set), it's O(1).

Space complexity: O(1) for the same reason. The positions vector can have up to 32 elements.

But in terms of Big O notation, since the loops run a constant number of times (32), the time and space complexity are O(1).

But for the problem's purposes, the code is efficient. So the efficiency is HIGH.

Another approach is to track the positions as we iterate through the bits. For example, iterate through each bit of n, and whenever a 1 is found, record its position. For example:

Initialize previous position as -infinity. For each bit, if it is 1, compute the difference with previous, update max. Then set previous to current.

This would avoid storing all positions. For example, in code:

int maxDiff = 0;
int prev = -1;
for (int i = 0; i < 32; ++i) {
    if (n & (1 << i)) {
        if (prev != -1) {
            int diff = i - prev;
            if (diff > maxDiff) {
                maxDiff = diff;
            }
        }
        prev = i;
    }
}
return maxDiff;

This approach uses O(1) space (only variables) and O(1) time. Which is better in terms of space. Let's see for n=22:

prev starts as -1.

i=0: no bit set. prev remains -1.

i=1: bit set. prev is -1. So set prev to 1. maxDiff remains 0.

i=2: bit set. now, i=2, prev=1. diff is 1. maxDiff is 1. set prev to 2.

i=3: no bit.

i=4: bit set. diff is 4-2=2. max becomes 2. prev is 4.

Other i's: no bits. So returns 2. Correct.

This approach is better in terms of space. So perhaps better to implement this way.

So the code can be optimized to not use a vector, but track previous positions. Let's code this.

In code:

int binaryGap(int n) {
    int maxDiff = 0;
    int prev = -1;
    for (int i = 0; i < 32; ++i) {
        if (n & (1 << i)) {
            if (prev != -1) {
                int diff = i - prev;
                if (diff > maxDiff) {
                    maxDiff = diff;
                }
            }
            prev = i;
        }
    }
    return maxDiff;
}

Wait, but what about for numbers where the highest bit is beyond 31? Well, since n is up to 1e9, which is less than 2^30, so 30 bits. So looping up to 31 is sufficient. So the code can loop up to 32 bits.

But in this code, for example, for n=5 (binary 101), the first 1 is at i=0. prev is -1, so no difference. Then prev is set to 0. Next 1 is at i=2. diff is 2-0=2. maxDiff is 2. So correct.

This approach is better in terms of space and time. Because it's O(1) space and O(1) time. So this is better.

So why didn't I think of this earlier? Because the first approach uses a vector, but this approach is more efficient.

So this approach works. And it's better in terms of space and time. So this is the better approach.

So the code can be written as follows.

Now, in C++, the loop can be written as for (int i = 0; i < 32; ++i). But perhaps we can find the highest bit and stop earlier. But for code simplicity, looping all 32 bits is acceptable.

So the code for the function is:

class Solution {
public:
    int binaryGap(int n) {
        int maxDiff = 0;
        int prev = -1;
        for (int i = 0; i < 32; ++i) {
            if (n & (1 << i)) {
                if (prev != -1) {
                    int diff = i - prev;
                    if (diff > maxDiff) {
                        maxDiff = diff;
                    }
                }
                prev = i;
            }
        }
        return maxDiff;
    }
};

Wait, but what about leading zeros? For example, i runs up to 31. But for numbers up to 1e9, this is okay.

Yes, this code works.

Now, what about the time and space complexity?

Time complexity: O(1) since the loop runs 32 times regardless of n.

Space complexity: O(1), only variables used.

Efficiency is HIGH.

Energy implications? Well, since it's O(1) time and space, it's very efficient. Energy implications are LOW.

So the code is correct and efficient.

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    int binaryGap(int n) {
        int maxDiff = 0;
        int prev = -1;
        for (int i = 0; i < 32; ++i) {
            if (n & (1 << i)) {
                if (prev != -1) {
                    int diff = i - prev;
                    if (diff > maxDiff) {
                        maxDiff = diff;
                    }
                }
                prev = i;
            }
        }
        return maxDiff;
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(1)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: This solution efficiently tracks the positions of 1s in the binary representation of `n` as it iter
